name: Psittaculidae
description: Psittaculidae is a diverse family of Old World parrots that includes lorikeets, lories, lovebirds, and parakeets, distributed across Africa, Asia, and Australasia.

children.name: Agapornis
children.description: Small, stocky, brightly-coloured parrots native to Africa and Madagascar, named for their strong pair bonds and affectionate behaviour. Lovebirds sleep side by side and form some of the most enduring pair bonds in the bird world.

children.name: Agapornis roseicollis
children.description: A small, vividly coloured parrot native to the arid regions of Namibia, South Africa, and Angola. It has a bright green body, a blue rump, and a distinctive rosy-pink face and throat. It is one of the most popular pet parrots worldwide, known for its affectionate nature and high intelligence.

children.name: Alisterus
children.description: A genus of large, long-tailed parrots with brilliant red and green plumage, found in forests from the Moluccas to Australia and New Guinea.

children.name: Alisterus scapularis
children.description: A striking large parrot with a red head and breast, green wings, and a long tail, found in rainforests and wet eucalypt forests of eastern Australia.

children.name: Alisterus amboinensis
children.description: The Moluccan king parrot has a red head and underparts, blue back and wings, and a long tail, found in forests of the Moluccas and western Papuan islands.

children.name: Alisterus chloropterus
children.description: The Papuan king parrot has a red head and breast, green wings, and a blue back, found in montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Aprosmictus
children.description: A genus of medium-sized parrots with distinctive red wing patches, found in woodlands and savannas of Australia and the Lesser Sundas.

children.name: Aprosmictus erythropterus
children.description: The red-winged parrot has a bright green body with striking red wing patches and a pale bill, found in open woodlands and savannas across northern and eastern Australia.

children.name: Chalcopsitta
children.description: A genus of medium-sized lories with predominantly dark plumage and brightly coloured bare facial skin, found in New Guinea and nearby islands.

children.name: Chalcopsitta atra
children.description: The black lory is mostly black with a dark red forehead and yellow undertail coverts, endemic to the Vogelkop region of northwestern New Guinea.

children.name: Chalcopsitta duivenbodei
children.description: The brown lory has dark brown plumage with yellow markings on the face and thighs, found in lowland forests of northern New Guinea.

children.name: Chalcopsitta scintillata
children.description: The yellowish lory has olive-yellow plumage with red facial skin and blue markings, found in lowland forests of southern New Guinea and the Aru Islands.

children.name: Charmosyna
children.description: A genus of small to medium-sized lorikeets with long, pointed tails and predominantly green plumage with colourful markings, found across New Guinea, the Moluccas, and the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Charmosyna josefinae
children.description: Josephine's lorikeet has a red body, green wings, and a black cap with violet-blue nape, found in montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Charmosyna papou
children.description: The Papuan lorikeet has a red body, green wings, and very long tail streamers, found in montane forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Charmosyna stellae
children.description: Stella's lorikeet has a red body, purple-blue belly, and long tail streamers, found in highland forests of central New Guinea.

children.name: Charmosyna rubrigularis
children.description: The red-chinned lorikeet has a green body, blue crown, and a distinctive red patch on the chin, found from the Bismarck Archipelago to the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Charmosyna palmarum
children.description: The palm lorikeet is mostly green with a red forehead, yellow underparts, and a long pointed tail, found in lowland forests and coconut plantations of the Solomon Islands and Vanuatu.

children.name: Cyclopsitta
children.description: A genus of tiny, short-tailed parrots with disproportionately large bills, specialised for feeding on figs and other fruits, found in New Guinea and Australia.

children.name: Cyclopsitta diophthalma
children.description: The double-eyed fig parrot is a tiny green parrot with a red forehead and blue facial markings, found in lowland forests of New Guinea and northeastern Australia.

children.name: Cyclopsitta melanogenia
children.description: The dusky fig parrot has a green body with a dark brown face and a pale bill, found in lowland forests of southern New Guinea.

children.name: Eclectus
children.description: A monotypic genus famous for extreme sexual dimorphism: the male is bright green with a coral-red bill, while the female is vivid red and purple with a black bill. For nearly a century, the two sexes were thought to be entirely different species.

children.name: Eclectus roratus
children.description: A large, stocky parrot found from the Moluccas and New Guinea to the Solomon Islands and northern Australia. The male is a brilliant emerald green with red and blue wing patches; the female is a striking crimson and purple.

children.name: Eos
children.description: A genus of brilliantly red lories with contrasting blue, purple, or black markings, found in the Moluccas and Wallacea. They are among the most stunningly coloured of all birds.

children.name: Eos bornea
children.description: The red lory is brilliant scarlet-red all over with blue flight feathers and a red bill, endemic to the Moluccas. It is a popular aviary bird known for its intelligence and playful nature.

children.name: Eos reticulata
children.description: The blue-streaked lory is bright red with distinctive blue streaks on the nape and chest, endemic to the Tanimbar Islands in Indonesia. It is considered vulnerable due to trapping for the pet trade.

children.name: Eos squamata
children.description: The violet-necked lory has a red body, violet-blue nape and belly, and black flight feathers, found across the Moluccas and Raja Ampat Islands.

children.name: Loriculus
children.description: The hanging parrots: a unique genus of tiny parrots that habitually sleep hanging upside down from branches. They are found across South and Southeast Asia, with brilliant green plumage and bright red or orange throat patches.

children.name: Loriculus galgulus
children.description: The blue-crowned hanging parrot has a green body with a distinctive blue crown patch and red rump, found in lowland forests of Thailand, Malaysia, Sumatra, and Borneo.

children.name: Loriculus vernalis
children.description: The vernal hanging parrot is a small green parrot with a red bill and orange throat patch, found from the Western Ghats of India to Indochina.

children.name: Loriculus beryllinus
children.description: The Sri Lanka hanging parrot is a small green parrot with a red crown, orange bill, and red rump, endemic to the forests of Sri Lanka.

children.name: Lorius
children.description: A genus of stocky, vividly coloured lories with blunt tails and a raucous, piercing call, found across New Guinea and the Moluccas. They are among the most brilliantly plumaged of all parrots, combining deep reds, blues, and yellows.

children.name: Lorius domicella
children.description: The purple-naped lory has a vivid red body, green wings, and a distinctive purple patch on the nape. It is endemic to the island of Seram in the Moluccas and is considered vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Lorius lory
children.description: The black-capped lory has a black cap, red body, and green wings with yellow bars, found across New Guinea and western Papuan islands. It is one of the most commonly kept lories in captivity.

children.name: Lorius chlorocercus
children.description: The yellow-bibbed lory has a red body, green wings, a black cap, and a broad yellow band across the upper breast, endemic to the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Lorius garrulus
children.description: The chattering lory is a striking red bird with green wings and yellow markings, named for its loud, chattering calls, endemic to the Moluccas in Indonesia.

children.name: Melopsittacus
children.description: A monotypic genus of small, social parrot that has become one of the most popular pet birds in the world. In the wild, Budgerigars are nomadic, following rainfall and food availability across the vast Australian interior.

children.name: Melopsittacus undulatus
children.description: A small, green-and-yellow parrot endemic to Australia's arid interior, where it occurs in huge nomadic flocks. It has a distinctive undulating flight pattern and a chattering, musical call.

children.name: Pseudeos
children.description: A small genus of medium-sized lories with duller plumage than most other lories, found in New Guinea and surrounding islands.

children.name: Pseudeos fuscata
children.description: The dusky lory has dark brown plumage with striking yellow and orange patches on the underparts and wings, found across New Guinea and nearby islands.

children.name: Pseudeos cardinalis
children.description: The cardinal lory has bright red plumage with black flight feathers and a dark bill, found in lowland forests of the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands.

children.name: Psephotus
children.description: Small, slender parrots with long tails and a semi-terrestrial foraging habit, endemic to Australia. They are known for their gentle, musical calls and beautiful plumage.

children.name: Psephotus haematonotus
children.description: A common and beautiful parrot of south-eastern Australia, found in open woodlands, grasslands, and urban parks. The male has a bright green body with a yellow belly and a distinctive red patch on the rump.

children.name: Psittacula
children.description: Slender, long-tailed parakeets with a distinctive ring or collar around the neck, found across Africa and Asia. They are highly adaptable birds, often thriving in urban environments well beyond their natural range.

children.name: Psittacula eupatria
children.description: One of the largest parakeets, found from Sri Lanka and India through Southeast Asia to Indochina. It has a striking green body, a massive red bill, and a distinctive pink-and-black collar.

children.name: Psittacula krameri
children.description: The most widely distributed parrot species, naturally ranging across Africa and Asia, with established feral populations in Europe, North America, and Japan. It has a bright green body, a red bill, and a distinctive black-and-pink collar.

children.name: Psittaculirostris
children.description: A genus of small, stocky fig parrots with brightly coloured plumage and large bills, found in lowland forests of New Guinea and nearby islands.

children.name: Psittaculirostris desmarestii
children.description: The large fig parrot has a green body with bright yellow and red markings on the head and breast, found in lowland forests of New Guinea.

children.name: Psittaculirostris edwardsii
children.description: Edwards's fig parrot has a green body with a red crown, yellow throat, and blue ear patch, found in lowland forests of northern New Guinea.

children.name: Trichoglossus
children.description: Vividly coloured, medium-sized parrots with a long, narrow bill and a specialised brush-tipped tongue adapted for feeding on nectar and pollen. Lorikeets are among the most brilliantly plumaged of all parrots.

children.name: Trichoglossus haematodus
children.description: One of the most colourful and widely recognised parrots, found from Indonesia and the Philippines through New Guinea to eastern Australia. Its plumage is a spectacular combination of blue head, green wings, yellow collar, and red breast.

children.name: Trichoglossus rubritorquis
children.description: The red-collared lorikeet is distinguished from the rainbow lorikeet by a broad orange-red band across the nape, found in northern Australia and nearby Indonesian islands.

children.name: Trichoglossus chlorolepidotus
children.description: The scaly-breasted lorikeet has a distinctive scalloped yellow-and-green breast pattern, endemic to eastern Australia where it frequents flowering eucalypts.

children.name: Trichoglossus ornatus
children.description: The ornate lorikeet has a green body with a yellow nape, red breast, and blue throat, endemic to Sulawesi and adjacent islands in Indonesia.

children.name: Trichoglossus forsteni
children.description: The sunset lorikeet has a green body with a yellow collar, red breast, and purple-blue belly, found in the Lesser Sunda Islands of Indonesia.

children.name: Trichoglossus weberi
children.description: The leaf lorikeet is mostly green with a yellow nape and blue throat, found in montane forests of Flores and Sumbawa in the Lesser Sundas.

children.name: Vini
children.description: A genus of small, active lorikeets with striking blue and green plumage, found in the Pacific Islands. Their name comes from the Tahitian word for a local parrot.

children.name: Vini rubrigularis
children.description: The red-chinned lorikeet has a green body, blue crown, and a distinctive red patch on the chin, found from the Bismarck Archipelago to the Solomon Islands.

children.name: Vini australis
children.description: The blue-crowned lorikeet has a green body with a blue crown, red breast, and red undertail coverts, found in Samoa, Tonga, and Fiji.

children.name: Vini kuhlii
children.description: Kuhl's lorikeet has a green body with a red head and breast, blue crown, and red-and-blue underparts, found in the Austral Islands and Kiribati.

children.name: Vini stepheni
children.description: Stephen's lorikeet has a green body with a red crown, red throat, and blue belly patch, endemic to Henderson Island in the Pitcairn group.
